opportunities.name: Massive productivity gains across knowledge work
opportunities.detail: Coding, legal, finance, marketing tasks automated or augmented. GDP boost of 1–3% in developed economies.

outcomes.text: Corporate earnings expand 10–20% in AI-adopting firms, driving equity valuations higher
Wage growth decouples from productivity — gains accrue to capital owners and top-tier talent
Smaller companies compete with enterprises for the first time, fragmenting market share
Countries that adopt fastest pull further ahead economically vs. laggards

opportunities.commentary: AI adoption has reached 55% of the U.S. population and 37% of workers as of August 2025, according to Federal Reserve research. Yet the productivity payoff remains elusive at the macro level — Goldman Sachs calculated AI's direct contribution to GDP growth was 'basically zero' after adjusting for imported hardware. Penn Wharton projects a cumulative 1.5% GDP boost by 2035, with peak annual contribution of just 0.2 percentage points in 2032. The disconnect between firm-level adoption and aggregate productivity echoes the IT productivity paradox of the 1990s. Probability is high because adoption is confirmed; the uncertainty is in timing and magnitude of the payoff. The outsized scenario assumes the J-curve resolves faster than historical precedent suggests.

opportunities.name: New trillion-dollar AI-native industries
opportunities.detail: Autonomous agents, AI infrastructure, synthetic media, personalized education become standalone sectors.

outcomes.text: Massive new job categories emerge — AI trainers, prompt engineers, agent designers
VC cycles shift — traditional SaaS investing declines, AI-native funding dominates
New monopoly risks as AI-native platforms achieve network effects faster than Web 2.0
GDP composition shifts — AI services become 5–10% of developed economy GDP by 2032

opportunities.commentary: Hyperscalers are projected to spend $660-690 billion on AI infrastructure in 2026, nearly doubling 2025 levels. AI-native platforms are commanding 25-30x EV/Revenue multiples versus 6-7x for traditional SaaS, signaling market belief in genuinely new value creation. The emergence of autonomous agents, AI-native coding tools, and synthetic media platforms represents early evidence of standalone sectors forming. Probability was nudged up from the original because early sector formation is now visible, though trillion-dollar scale remains years away.

opportunities.name: Reshoring of manufacturing via robotics + AI
opportunities.detail: AI-driven automation makes domestic manufacturing cost-competitive again.

outcomes.text: Blue-collar job creation in robot maintenance and factory ops — but fewer total jobs than offshored
Geopolitical leverage of low-cost labor countries diminishes
Real estate boom in US industrial corridors near energy sources
National security posture improves with domestic supply chains

opportunities.commentary: The tariff environment has accelerated reshoring interest — the U.S. trade deficit fell to its lowest level since 2009 in late 2025 as businesses paused imports. AI-driven robotics could make domestic manufacturing cost-competitive, but the timeline is long and capital-intensive. Probability remains moderate because the physical buildout lags software adoption by years. The outsized scenario envisions a convergence of trade policy, robotics advancement, and energy availability that could dramatically accelerate the timeline.

opportunities.name: Energy sector supercycle from AI demand
opportunities.detail: Data center buildout drives massive investment in power generation, grid modernization, nuclear, and renewables.

outcomes.text: Utility stocks become growth investments, not just yield plays
Nuclear renaissance accelerates — SMRs and fusion attract tens of billions
Grid bottlenecks create regional winners and losers
Climate goals face tension as natural gas fills the gap

opportunities.commentary: A critical signal: US shale producers are NOT adding new drilling despite oil briefly touching $120. They view the spike as temporary/war-driven, not structural. This means the energy supercycle thesis depends on hyperscaler-driven demand (intact at $645B capex), not oil prices. The Saudi Red Sea pipeline offer and G7 reserve mechanism provide partial supply buffers. BlackRock expects disruptions to last 'weeks not months.' But Iraq's output collapse (-60%), desalination plant attacks, and Iran's vow to block all oil create execution uncertainty. If the war ends in weeks (Trump's signal), the energy infrastructure investment thesis recovers quickly. If it drags to 8 weeks (Hegseth's estimate), structural damage to Gulf infrastructure resets timelines. Probability maintained at [65, 85].

opportunities.name: Semiconductor industry sustained boom
opportunities.detail: Insatiable compute demand keeps semis growing at 15–25% CAGR.

outcomes.text: NVIDIA, TSMC sustain premium valuations — semi index outperforms broader market
New entrants in custom silicon fragment GPU dominance over time
Geopolitical risk around Taiwan becomes the single biggest tail risk
CAPEX arms race among hyperscalers reaches $100B+/year collectively

opportunities.commentary: Remarkably, the semiconductor boom continues to defy the macro selloff. On March 9, with the Dow down 900 points at lows and oil touching $120, Broadcom gained 3%, Micron +2%, AMD +1%. Semiconductors were one of only 4 Dow components in the green. Broadcom's AI revenue +106% YoY and Marvell's record design wins from last week continue to anchor the thesis. Aggregate hyperscaler capex at $645B for 2026. The stress test is: if oil stays above $100 for 4+ weeks, does enterprise AI demand destruction eventually flow back to reduce infrastructure orders? Not yet, but this is the key question. Probability maintained at [87, 96] because the earnings data is unambiguous, but the macro environment is now testing whether this can persist.

risks.name: Asset bubble and violent correction in AI stocks
risks.detail: Hype outpaces near-term revenue realization. A 30–50% drawdown is plausible.

outcomes.text: Broader market contagion — AI stocks now 30%+ of S&P 500
Startup funding winter — down rounds, consolidation wave begins
Strong AI companies with real revenue emerge as long-term winners at discounted valuations
Public backlash against 'AI hype' narrative

risks.commentary: The market is not crashing — it's experiencing the most extreme intraday volatility since April. Monday: S&P fell 1.5% then REVERSED to close +0.8% after Trump's 'very complete' remarks — the first intraday reversal of that magnitude since April. Tuesday: S&P +0.4%, Dow +244. Oil VIX above 100 (highest since April 2020). But the broader picture is grim: Nasdaq below 200-day MA, YTD losses at ~5%. Yardeni's 35% crash probability. Financial sector under severe stress: XLF -4.5% in 3 days, KRE -7%, private credit concerns around Blue Owl and BlackRock withdrawal limits. Bond basis trades unwinding (Peccatiello: 'when you attack the foundation of financial markets — the bond market — things can get nasty quickly'). The market is priced for a SHORT war. If Hegseth's '8 weeks' or Iran's vow to block all oil materializes, the correction deepens into crash. If Trump's 'very complete' proves accurate, recovery is sharp. Binary outcome. Probability moderated 2 points from yesterday because the stabilization signals are real, but the range remains extreme.

risks.name: Structural unemployment in white-collar sectors
risks.detail: Mid-level knowledge workers face displacement faster than retraining occurs.

outcomes.text: Political pressure for UBI or expanded safety nets grows dramatically
Housing markets in high-cost knowledge-worker cities face demand pressure
Education industry disrupted — traditional degrees lose value
Consumer spending contraction slows GDP growth by 0.5–1%

risks.commentary: The structural unemployment thesis is now operating in a stagflationary environment. The -92K BLS report was bad enough in isolation. Now add $100+ oil: companies facing simultaneous margin pressure from energy costs AND AI-driven restructuring incentives will accelerate workforce reductions. Block's playbook (cut 40% of workforce, stock surges 24%) becomes even more attractive when energy inflation squeezes margins. The cruise industry is already shedding — Carnival and Norwegian down 20%+ creates pressure for layoffs. Airlines facing margin compression from jet fuel. The UN declared the Iran war a 'major humanitarian emergency' affecting 25M refugees — the economic disruption extends far beyond US borders. Probability increased because the stagflationary overlay makes AI-driven workforce reductions more likely, not less.

risks.name: Concentration of wealth in a few AI megacorps
risks.detail: Winner-take-most dynamics in foundation models.

outcomes.text: Antitrust action intensifies globally
Innovation bottleneck — startups can't compete on compute
Corporate lobbying power rivals Big Oil and Big Pharma at their peaks
Index fund investors benefit as megacap AI stocks dominate market cap

risks.commentary: The top six AI companies account for roughly 30% of the S&P 500. Hyperscaler capex alone approaches $700 billion in 2026. Winner-take-most dynamics are confirmed in foundation models where training costs create natural barriers. The SaaS market is bifurcating into AI-native platforms (25-30x multiples) and legacy software (6-7x and falling). 80% of PE and strategic buyers report a valuation uplift for AI-native companies, further concentrating value. The outsized scenario envisions these companies wielding political influence rivaling historical industry giants.

risks.name: Inflation in energy & compute costs
risks.detail: AI power consumption strains grids, raising costs for consumers and non-AI businesses.

outcomes.text: Non-AI businesses face margin compression from rising electricity costs
Political backlash against data centers in local communities
Massive incentive for efficiency breakthroughs in AI compute
Renewable energy and battery storage investments accelerate
Regional inequality widens based on power costs

risks.commentary: Oil has pulled back sharply from $120 to ~$88 on three stabilization signals: G7 asked IEA to study emergency reserve releases (400M barrels), Saudi offered Red Sea pipeline bypassing Hormuz, and Trump told CBS the war is 'very complete, pretty much.' But the pullback coexists with escalation: Hegseth announced March 10 would be 'the most intense day of strikes yet,' Iran vowed not to allow 'one liter of oil' to leave the Middle East until attacks cease, and IRGC rejected claims its missile program was destroyed. The 52-week range is now $55 to $119 — extreme. Gas heading to $4/gallon per OPIS. US shale producers are NOT adding new drilling despite $100+ prices — signaling they view the spike as temporary and war-driven, not fundamentally structural. Iraq output collapsed 60%. Desalination plants attacked (threatening water in countries getting 90% of supply from desalination). Probability moderated slightly from yesterday's peak because the G7 reserve mechanism and Saudi pipeline provide partial buffers, but remains near-ceiling because the underlying Hormuz closure and war escalation continue.

risks.name: Emerging market destabilization
risks.detail: Countries reliant on outsourced knowledge work face economic disruption.

outcomes.text: Political instability — youth unemployment drives social unrest
Migration pressure increases to AI-leading economies
Some emerging markets pivot — India's AI talent becomes its new export
Global demand contraction from reduced purchasing power

risks.commentary: Emerging markets are in acute distress. South Korea's Kospi has triggered two circuit breakers in 4 sessions, with its worst week since March 2020. Samsung -10%, SK Hynix -11.6%. Asia imports 90% of oil that passes through Hormuz. India braces for selloff. The UN declared the war a 'major humanitarian emergency' affecting 25M refugees. Oil-importing emerging markets face simultaneous currency pressure (dollar rising), energy cost inflation, and disrupted supply chains. The cascading effects on global AI adoption, offshore development teams, and emerging market tech sectors could be significant. Probability increased.

opportunities.name: AI-native startups displace incumbents
opportunities.detail: Startups building AI-first products leapfrog legacy SaaS.

outcomes.text: Massive M&A wave as incumbents acquire AI-native competitors
Enterprise software market fragments — best-of-breed AI tools replace suites
Salesforce, Adobe, ServiceNow face existential pressure
New unicorns emerge in 12–18 months vs. 5–7 year traditional cycle

opportunities.commentary: The displacement dynamic is more nuanced than 'AI-native startups replace incumbents.' The SaaSpocalypse created a bifurcated market, but the bifurcation runs through incumbents as much as between incumbents and startups. The strongest incumbents — those with distribution advantages, data gravity, regulatory moats, and complex workflow orchestration — are playing offense: embedding AI as an upsell, shifting to outcome-based pricing that expands contract values even as seat counts shrink, and using their installed base to cross-sell AI-native capabilities into adjacent TAM. ServiceNow, Palantir, and Veeva are examples of incumbents that are effectively becoming AI-native from within. Block's internal AI tool 'Goose' is a case study in build-over-buy, but it also demonstrates that companies with engineering depth can self-disrupt rather than waiting to be disrupted. Meanwhile, pure AI-native startups face their own challenges: customer acquisition costs remain high, enterprise procurement cycles are slow, and the IPO window for SaaS companies has frozen. The build-vs-buy pendulum is swinging toward build as coding agents lower barriers, but incumbents with existing customer relationships and compliance infrastructure have a head start in regulated verticals. The correct framing is not 'startups vs. incumbents' but 'companies that successfully transition to AI-native economics vs. those that don't' — and that cuts across both categories. Probability maintained because displacement is real and accelerating, but the winners include both AI-native startups and offensive-minded incumbents.

opportunities.name: 10x developer productivity reshapes team sizes
opportunities.detail: Small teams of 5–10 build what required 50–100.

outcomes.text: Software company headcount flatlines even as revenue grows — margin expansion
Solo founders build $10M+ ARR businesses, reshaping venture economics
Senior engineers command premium salaries as 'AI multipliers'
Speed of iteration accelerates — competitive moats shrink from years to months

opportunities.commentary: Claude Code, Cursor, GitHub Copilot, and similar tools are widely adopted and demonstrably accelerating development speed. Solo founders are building $10M+ ARR businesses. Software company headcounts are flatlining while revenue grows, confirming margin expansion. The probability range was widened upward because the tools exist and adoption is confirmed — the uncertainty is in how far the productivity multiplier extends beyond early adopters. Senior engineers commanding premium salaries as 'AI multipliers' is already observable in compensation data.

opportunities.name: Vertical AI agents create new SaaS categories
opportunities.detail: Industry-specific autonomous agents become the new dominant software paradigm.

outcomes.text: Billing shifts from seats to tasks/outcomes — one agent replaces 5–10 seats
Domain expertise becomes the moat, not engineering
Compliance and liability frameworks lag — lawsuits shape the legal landscape
Total addressable market for software expands 3–5x

opportunities.commentary: The Claude Cowork launch and Plugin Event (January 30, 2026) confirmed vertical AI agents as the most disruptive force in enterprise software. Anthropic's 11 open-source specialized plugins for legal, tax, sales, and CRM workflows triggered the SaaSpocalypse. Billing is shifting from seats to tasks/outcomes. MCP (Model Context Protocol) has become the de facto standard for agent-to-tool communication, with OpenAI, Microsoft, and Google embracing it; Anthropic donated it to the Linux Foundation's Agentic AI Foundation. NVIDIA's $4 billion investment in photonics signals infrastructure positioning for agent-scale compute demands. Gartner predicts 35% of point-product SaaS tools replaced by AI agents by 2030, but markets price in faster adoption. Domain expertise is confirmed as the primary moat. Probability was increased because market validation of the thesis is now unambiguous.

opportunities.name: Usage-based AI pricing expands TAM
opportunities.detail: Shift from seat-based to outcome-based pricing.

outcomes.text: Revenue per customer increases 2–5x for high-value workflows
Revenue volatility increases — usage fluctuates with cycles
CFOs push back on uncapped AI spend
New metrics emerge replacing traditional SaaS metrics

opportunities.commentary: The shift from seat-based to usage/outcome-based pricing is accelerating as the mechanism through which incumbents capture expanded TAM. CFOs still push back on uncapped AI spend, but the conversation has shifted from 'whether' to 'how' — with hybrid models (base platform fee + outcome-based AI tier) emerging as the compromise. ServiceNow, Salesforce (despite stock declines), and HubSpot are all piloting outcome-based pricing tiers. The key insight is that outcome-based pricing aligns vendor incentives with customer value creation, which makes it the natural model for AI-expanded workflows where the value per interaction is high but unpredictable. New metrics are emerging to replace traditional SaaS benchmarks (ARR, NRR) — 'revenue per workflow automated' and 'cost per outcome' are gaining traction with analysts. Probability increased because the pricing transition is now a competitive necessity, not an experiment, and the hybrid models are resolving the CFO pushback that previously suppressed adoption.

opportunities.name: Open-source AI democratizes software creation
opportunities.detail: Non-developers build functional apps via AI.

outcomes.text: Long tail of niche micro-SaaS products explodes
Quality and security concerns rise from non-engineer-built software
Low-code platforms get leapfrogged by AI code generation
Engineering evolves from 'writing code' to 'designing systems'

opportunities.commentary: Vibe coding tools have made it possible for non-developers to build functional applications. Low-code platforms like Bubble and Retool are being leapfrogged by AI code generation. The long tail of niche micro-SaaS is expanding. However, quality and security concerns from non-engineer-built software represent a real counterweight. Probability was increased because the tools are mature and accessible, though the outsized scenario assumes these applications achieve enterprise-grade reliability.

opportunities.name: AI collapses implementation costs, expanding addressable workflows
opportunities.detail: AI agents and copilots reduce the cost of automating complex workflows by 5-10x, making software economically viable for millions of small and mid-market businesses, back-office functions, and niche verticals that were never part of traditional SaaS TAM.

outcomes.text: Total software TAM expands 30-60% as previously unaddressable workflows become viable
Incumbents with existing distribution channels and vertical expertise capture disproportionate share of new TAM
SMB and mid-market adoption accelerates as AI reduces implementation time from months to days
New TAM partially offsets seat compression — fewer seats per customer but more customers and deeper workflow penetration
Quality and reliability concerns slow adoption in regulated verticals (healthcare, legal, finance)
Incumbents with sales teams, compliance infrastructure, and customer trust have structural advantage over startups in capturing new TAM

opportunities.commentary: The most underweighted dynamic in the previous model was the TAM expansion effect. AI doesn't just compress existing software economics — it creates entirely new addressable markets by collapsing the cost of automation. A 20-person law firm that could never justify a $50K/year practice management suite can now access equivalent functionality through AI agents at a fraction of the cost. A regional hospital's scheduling logic, a mid-market distributor's inventory optimization, a local government's permitting workflow — these were never SaaS TAM because the implementation cost (consultants, customization, change management) exceeded the value. AI agents collapse that cost structure by 5-10x, and the companies best positioned to capture this new TAM are often incumbents with existing distribution channels, vertical expertise, compliance certifications, and customer trust. ServiceNow expanding into mid-market, Veeva pushing into smaller biotech, Palantir moving from government into commercial verticals — these are examples of incumbents using AI to go downmarket and into adjacencies. The dynamic partially offsets seat compression: even if each customer has fewer human seats, the total number of customers and the depth of workflow penetration both increase. Gartner estimates that AI will expand the enterprise software TAM by 35-50% by 2030 through previously unaddressable use cases. The initial probability is set at [62, 84] because the implementation cost collapse is already measurable, but full TAM realization requires reliability improvements in regulated verticals and the completion of pricing model transitions. This opportunity is the primary counterweight to sw-risk-1 (margin compression) and sw-risk-3 (churn acceleration) — aggregate software sector revenue may grow even as per-seat economics shrink.

risks.name: SaaS margin compression from AI cost pass-through
risks.detail: Gross margins drop from 75–80% to 55–65% during transition.

outcomes.text: Public SaaS multiples contract from 10–15x to 6–9x revenue
Companies that optimize inference costs fastest gain structural advantages
Pressure to vertically integrate — build or fine-tune own models
Investors rotate out of 'AI wrapper' companies into infrastructure

risks.commentary: $100+ oil directly impacts SaaS margins through inference cost inflation. Data center electricity costs will spike. Every company passing AI costs through to customers faces pricing pressure from enterprise buyers whose own budgets are squeezed by energy inflation. The March 9 market action shows the dynamic: only energy and semiconductors held up; 75% of S&P issues declined. Application-layer SaaS is caught between rising costs and falling customer willingness to pay. TAM expansion (sw-opp-6) provides partial offset but the near-term margin impact of $100+ oil is severe. Probability increased.

risks.name: Commoditization of core software functions
risks.detail: AI makes it trivial to replicate features. Moats erode.

outcomes.text: Data moats become the primary competitive advantage
Brand and enterprise relationships matter more than features
Pricing races to the bottom for commodity features
Ecosystem lock-in replaces feature differentiation

risks.commentary: 80% of strategic and PE buyers cite AI-driven commoditization as the top risk to SaaS valuations. 64% of SaaS CEOs report that AI is already lowering barriers to entry in their markets, with another 20% expecting impact within two years. Small teams are building credible competitors in weeks rather than months. Data moats and ecosystem lock-in are replacing feature differentiation as the primary competitive advantages. The minimal scenario assumes gradual commoditization at the feature level; the outsized scenario assumes entire categories collapse into commodity pricing.

risks.name: Legacy SaaS churn acceleration
risks.detail: Enterprises replace bloated SaaS stacks with AI-native alternatives.

outcomes.text: Mass layoffs at legacy SaaS companies across all functions
Enterprise IT budgets reallocate from legacy to AI-native
AI integration consulting becomes a $50B+ market
Some legacy players pivot successfully and strengthen position

risks.commentary: The SaaSpocalypse repricing is real but increasingly differentiated. Nearly $1 trillion in software market value evaporated in early 2026, IGV entered a bear market, and forward multiples collapsed from 39x to 21x. The catalyst was Claude Cowork and the Plugin Event demonstrating AI agents in vertical workflows. Block's AI-driven layoff of 4,000 workers (stock +24%) validated the seat compression thesis. However, 'legacy SaaS churn' is concentrated, not uniform. Commodity point solutions — undifferentiated tools that AI agents replicate at minimal integration cost — face existential churn risk. But incumbents with systems-of-record status, regulated data, or deep workflow integration are using the disruption as a catalyst to expand into new TAM: automating previously unaddressable workflows, pushing downmarket via AI-reduced implementation costs, and cross-selling AI capabilities into adjacent verticals. The churn in legacy seats is partially offset by new customer acquisition and deeper workflow penetration. The credit risk in the $235B software loan market (~50% rated B- or lower) maps disproportionately to the long tail. The 2-4 quarter revenue transition valley remains a real near-term risk even for successful pivoters. Probability moderated slightly to reflect the TAM expansion offset, though it remains the highest software risk because the aggregate sector repricing continues.

risks.name: Platform dependency on foundation model providers
risks.detail: Existential risk if providers vertically integrate.

outcomes.text: Repeat of 'building on someone else's platform' — Zynga/Facebook dynamics
Multi-model strategies become mandatory
Open-source models gain adoption as a hedge
Foundation model providers face regulatory scrutiny

risks.commentary: The Zynga/Facebook dynamic is repeating as software companies build critical dependencies on foundation model providers. Multi-model strategies are becoming mandatory as a hedge against vertical integration risk. Open-source models (Meta's Llama, Mistral) are gaining adoption specifically as a platform dependency hedge. The probability is high because the dependency is structural and already locked in. Foundation model providers face regulatory scrutiny, but the timeline for meaningful regulation is longer than the window of competitive vulnerability.

risks.name: Software developer job market contraction
risks.detail: Junior dev hiring slows 30–50%. Senior roles shift to AI orchestration.

outcomes.text: CS enrollment declines — curricula pivot toward AI systems design
Offshore development shops face existential threat
Remaining developers command higher salaries
Open-source contribution declines — ecosystem health risk

risks.commentary: The ADP February report (+63K private jobs) provides a sector-level nuance to the developer job market picture. Construction and education/health led hiring — sectors less exposed to AI displacement. But the record-low job-changer pay premium suggests workers who do switch jobs are commanding less, consistent with employer leverage in the hiring process. January was revised to just 11K (from 22K). Block's 4,000 AI-driven layoffs remain the defining event, with stock surging 24% and CEO Dorsey predicting most companies will follow. Software/IT/math unemployment at 3.6%. Software engineers report 12+ month searches. The Citrini Research essay and Citadel Securities rebuttal continue to frame the debate. The Iran war adds macro uncertainty — if stagflation materializes, companies may accelerate AI-driven workforce reductions to protect margins against energy cost inflation. CrowdStrike and Box both reported strong earnings this week, suggesting that not all software companies are contracting — cybersecurity and intelligent content management remain growth areas even in the downturn. Probability maintained at [58, 78].

opportunities.name: Personalized education at scale
opportunities.detail: AI tutors adapt to individual learning styles.

outcomes.text: Achievement gaps narrow in underserved areas
Teaching roles evolve toward mentorship and facilitation
Standardized testing becomes obsolete
Screen time concerns increase for children

opportunities.commentary: AI tutoring tools are demonstrating measurable improvements in personalized learning outcomes. The opportunity to narrow achievement gaps in underserved communities is real, but adoption is uneven — well-resourced districts adopt faster, potentially widening the gap before narrowing it. Screen time concerns for children represent a genuine counterweight. The probability and impact ranges reflect the tension between transformative potential and implementation challenges in education systems that are structurally resistant to change.

opportunities.name: Scientific discovery acceleration
opportunities.detail: AI accelerates drug discovery, materials science, climate research.

outcomes.text: New materials for energy storage and carbon capture discovered faster
Scientific publishing overwhelmed — peer review breaks down
Biotech valuations surge with AI-discovered drug candidates
Research becomes capital-intensive — smaller labs fall behind

opportunities.commentary: AI is accelerating discovery across drug development, materials science, and climate research with confirmed results. AlphaFold-class breakthroughs in protein structure prediction are enabling previously impossible research directions. Biotech valuations are surging on the back of AI-discovered drug candidates. The St. Louis Fed confirms AI investment in R&D is already showing up in GDP data. However, scientific publishing is being overwhelmed — the peer review system is straining under the volume of AI-assisted research. Probability is high because acceleration is demonstrated; the outsized scenario assumes compounding returns as AI-discovered insights feed back into further AI capability improvement.

opportunities.name: Accessibility revolution for disabled populations
opportunities.detail: Real-time translation, vision aids, cognitive assistance tools.

outcomes.text: Workforce participation among disabled populations increases
Assistive technology market grows from ~$30B to $100B+
Digital accessibility standards rewritten around AI
Dependency on AI tools creates fragility for disabled users

opportunities.commentary: Real-time translation, computer vision aids, and cognitive assistance tools are enabling participation for disabled populations in ways previously impossible. The assistive technology market is projected to grow from roughly $30 billion to over $100 billion. Workforce participation among disabled populations is increasing with accessible AI tools. The risk of fragility — dependency on AI tools that could fail or be discontinued — is a genuine concern that tempers the probability range.

opportunities.name: Reduction in bureaucratic friction
opportunities.detail: AI streamlines government services, tax filing, legal processes.

outcomes.text: Tax compliance costs drop dramatically
Government headcount declines 20–30% — union pushback
Legal and gov services improve for low-income populations
AI-automated government decisions create due process challenges

opportunities.commentary: AI-streamlined government services represent one of the highest-impact but lowest-probability opportunities in the model. The potential for reducing bureaucratic friction in tax compliance, legal processes, and service delivery is enormous. However, government adoption lags private sector by years, union resistance is strong, and due process challenges from automated government decisions create legal barriers. The outsized scenario assumes a political mandate for government modernization that overcomes institutional resistance.

risks.name: Deepfake-driven erosion of shared reality
risks.detail: Trust in all media collapses as synthetic media becomes indistinguishable.

outcomes.text: Provenance verification tech becomes a new essential industry
Video/audio evidence becomes contestable in every legal case
'Liar's dividend' — real wrongdoing dismissed as deepfakes
Premium on trusted media brands increases

risks.commentary: The information integrity crisis is exemplified by the school strike investigation. Bellingcat verified video showing a US cruise missile striking a compound near the school — contradicting Trump's claims Iran struck its own school. But Trump said 'whatever the report shows, I'm willing to live with that.' Six Democratic senators called for investigation. The liar's dividend is fully operational: evidence-based investigation coexists with dismissal of evidence. Iran has been in a 240-hour internet blackout — the longest government-imposed shutdown on record. Every faction is generating competing narratives. Iranian women's soccer players granted asylum by Australia after silent anthem protest. The information environment makes any fact, including AI-generated content, essentially unverifiable for most consumers. Probability maintained at [88, 97] — near ceiling.

risks.name: Mass loneliness from AI companions
risks.detail: AI relationships substitute for human connection.

outcomes.text: Depression and anxiety rates climb in demographics reliant on AI companions
Birth rates decline further in developed nations
Counter-movement toward 'authentic human connection'
Elderly care costs potentially decrease

risks.commentary: AI companion products are growing but the societal impact remains speculative. The concern centers on substitution effects — AI relationships replacing human connection rather than supplementing it. Declining birth rates in developed nations could accelerate. A counter-movement toward 'authentic human connection' is already emerging. The probability range reflects genuine uncertainty about behavioral adoption patterns and whether AI companionship proves sticky or whether users naturally revert to human connection.

risks.name: Widening digital divide (AI-literate vs. non-literate)
risks.detail: Those who leverage AI pull dramatically ahead in productivity and income.

outcomes.text: AI-literate workers earn 2–3x more than peers in similar roles
'AI elites' vs. 'left behind' becomes defining social tension
Massive demand for AI literacy programs
Companies investing in AI training gain competitive advantages

risks.commentary: AI adoption is confirmed at 55% of the U.S. population but is unevenly distributed by income, education, and geography. Workers who leverage AI tools are already commanding salary premiums over peers in similar roles. Brookings identifies 6.1 million workers with high AI exposure and low adaptive capacity. The 'AI elites versus left behind' framing is already emerging in public discourse. Massive demand for AI literacy programs exists but supply is fragmented. Probability was increased because the divide is measurable; the outsized scenario models a self-reinforcing cycle where early AI adopters accumulate advantages that become structurally difficult to close.

risks.name: Collapse of creative industries' economic model
risks.detail: AI-generated content floods markets. Mid-tier creatives face severe income loss.

outcomes.text: Premium on 'human-made' creative work — but only for top-tier creators
Copyright law undergoes biggest revision since the internet
Content quality paradox — infinite 'good enough,' less truly great
New AI creative director roles emerge

risks.commentary: AI-generated content is flooding creative markets, compressing mid-tier creative incomes. A premium on 'human-made' creative work exists but only for top-tier creators. Copyright law is facing its biggest revision since the internet — multiple high-profile cases are working through courts. The 'content quality paradox' — infinite 'good enough' content but less truly great work — is becoming visible. New AI creative director roles are emerging, but the net effect on creative employment appears negative in the near term.

risks.name: Surveillance normalization
risks.detail: AI-powered monitoring becomes ubiquitous and socially accepted.

outcomes.text: Privacy becomes a luxury good
Employee monitoring reduces workplace autonomy
Crime rates may decrease but over-policing intensifies
Privacy tech becomes a growth industry

risks.commentary: AI-powered monitoring is becoming ubiquitous across workplaces, public spaces, and online platforms. The normalization of surveillance is proceeding faster in some societies than others. Privacy is increasingly becoming a luxury good — those who can afford to opt out of surveillance systems do so, while others cannot. Employee monitoring is measurably reducing workplace autonomy. Privacy technology is emerging as a growth industry in response, but the asymmetry between surveillance capability and privacy protection continues to widen.

opportunities.name: AI-enhanced government efficiency
opportunities.detail: Automated regulatory analysis, fraud detection, resource allocation.

outcomes.text: Tax fraud detection improves — billions in savings annually
Government responsiveness improves — days not months
Risk of opaque 'algorithmic bureaucracy'
Public sector tech talent gap widens

opportunities.commentary: AI-enhanced government efficiency faces the widest gap between potential and probability in the model. Tax fraud detection, resource allocation optimization, and automated regulatory analysis could save billions annually. However, government IT modernization has a poor track record, the public sector tech talent gap is widening, and the risk of opaque 'algorithmic bureaucracy' creates political resistance. The outsized scenario assumes a crisis (fiscal, pandemic, or security) that creates political will for rapid government AI adoption.

opportunities.name: International AI safety cooperation frameworks
opportunities.detail: Shared concern drives multilateral coordination.

outcomes.text: Global standards reduce race-to-the-bottom dynamics
Enforcement challenges — AI capabilities hard to verify
Smaller nations gain voice in AI governance
'AI safety premium' in markets for compliant nations

opportunities.commentary: International AI safety cooperation prospects have deteriorated further. The December 2025 executive order explicitly prioritizes competitive dominance over multilateral coordination. The AI Litigation Task Force focuses on eliminating domestic regulatory 'barriers' rather than building international frameworks. U.S.-China AI competition fragments the global ecosystem. The EU delayed high-risk AI Act obligations from 2026 to 2027. The onset of the U.S.-Iran war further diverts diplomatic bandwidth from AI governance. OpenAI's amended Pentagon deal (no domestic surveillance of U.S. persons) and the Defense Department's public criticism of Anthropic's refusal to negotiate suggest military AI applications are outpacing governance frameworks. Probability was decreased because the political trajectory in the world's largest AI economy actively undermines multilateral cooperation.

opportunities.name: New regulatory models stimulate innovation
opportunities.detail: Smart regulation creates trust and enables responsible deployment.

outcomes.text: Regulatory sandboxes allow innovation while containing risk
Regulatory clarity attracts institutional investment
Compliance costs create barriers for startups
Countries with smart regulation attract AI talent

opportunities.commentary: The federal-state regulatory clash has made smart, coherent AI regulation less likely in the near term. The December 2025 executive order established an AI Litigation Task Force to challenge state AI laws, while conditioning $21 billion in broadband funding on states repealing AI regulations deemed 'onerous.' Multiple state AGs (Colorado, California) plan to challenge federal preemption in court. 280+ bipartisan state lawmakers signed a letter opposing federal preemption. Colorado's AI Act takes effect June 2026 amid uncertainty. The Iran war will further consume legislative attention. Probability decreased because the two-track reality (states regulating vs. federal preempting) creates confusion rather than clarity.

risks.name: AI-driven election manipulation at scale
risks.detail: Micro-targeted AI propaganda undermines democratic processes.

outcomes.text: Election legitimacy questioned in every major democracy
Platforms forced into aggressive content moderation
Voter cynicism deepens — turnout declines
Verified identity systems for political ads create new industry

risks.commentary: Election integrity during wartime is now a first-order crisis. War powers failed in Senate. Trump demands role in selecting Iran's next leader. AI Super PACs active in primaries. The wartime information environment (school strike debate, competing casualty narratives, NATO missile interception) creates infinite material for AI-generated disinformation. Iran-affiliated groups have strong motivation for retaliatory election interference. Midterms continue with no new federal AI guardrails. FEC deadlocked. Probability increased significantly.

risks.name: US-China AI cold war escalation
risks.detail: Compute export controls, IP theft, military AI race intensify.

outcomes.text: Two separate AI ecosystems — fragmented standards, duplicated costs
Allied nations forced to choose sides
Defense spending on autonomous weapons surges
Chinese tech companies develop competitive alternatives

risks.commentary: Compute export controls continue to intensify. Two separate AI ecosystems are forming along geopolitical lines. Allied nations face increasing pressure to choose sides. China has stated its goal to lead in AI by 2030 and is developing competitive alternatives across the stack. Defense spending on autonomous weapons systems is surging. The probability reflects the entrenched nature of the competition — the question is not whether it escalates but how far. The outsized scenario assumes a Taiwan contingency that forces a rapid, destabilizing decoupling.

risks.name: Authoritarian AI-powered social control
risks.detail: Non-democratic regimes deploy AI for comprehensive citizen monitoring.

outcomes.text: Democratic backsliding accelerates globally
Dissent becomes nearly impossible to organize
Pressure to restrict AI surveillance exports
Digital resistance and counter-surveillance tools emerge

risks.commentary: Non-democratic regimes are deploying AI surveillance at increasing scale and sophistication. Export of AI surveillance technology is growing. Dissent is becoming measurably harder to organize in countries with comprehensive AI monitoring. Democratic backsliding metrics are worsening globally. The probability range is wide because the trend is clear but the pace varies significantly by country. Digital resistance and counter-surveillance tools are emerging but asymmetrically disadvantaged against state-level resources.

risks.name: Regulatory capture by AI megacorps
risks.detail: Large companies shape regulation to entrench incumbents.

outcomes.text: Compliance complexity chills innovation
Revolving door erodes public trust in regulation
Open-source AI community mobilizes as counter-lobby
EU regulatory independence creates alternative innovation hubs

risks.commentary: Regulatory capture concerns have intensified as the December 2025 executive order's language closely mirrors industry positions on deregulation. The order explicitly criticizes Colorado's anti-algorithmic-discrimination law. Federal broadband funding is conditioned on states repealing AI regulations. The Defense Department publicly criticized Anthropic for not negotiating on military AI while praising OpenAI's cooperation — suggesting companies willing to align with government interests receive favorable treatment. Data center companies are spending heavily on local political engagement. Probability increased because structural incentives for capture are stronger when the regulatory posture is explicitly 'innovation-first.'

risks.name: Governance unable to keep pace with AI
risks.detail: De facto governance by corporate policy, not democratic process.

outcomes.text: Corporate ethics boards become actual regulators — unelected, unaccountable
Legal gray zones exploited aggressively
Incidents force rapid, reactive, poorly designed regulation
Self-regulatory industry bodies form with mixed effectiveness

risks.commentary: The governance crisis has a new dimension: the US government is sending contradictory signals to its own markets and allies. Trump: 'war is very complete, pretty much.' Hegseth (same day): 'most intense day of strikes yet.' Trump: war will end 'very soon.' Also Trump: 'haven't won enough, seeking ultimate victory.' Iran: 'Iran will determine when the war ends.' IRGC rejects claims its missile program is destroyed. This incoherence is itself a governance failure — markets are whipsawing on the contradictions (oil: $87 → $120 → $88 in 36 hours). The Commerce Dept AI regulation deadline (March 11) is TOMORROW and completely invisible. CPI Wednesday. Fed March 18. Iran 240-hour internet blackout. UNESCO World Heritage sites damaged (Naqsh-e Jahan, Chehel Sotoun, Golestan Palace). The governance system cannot process domestic policy while generating contradictory war signals. Probability maintained at [88, 98].

risks.name: Data-center and energy backlash slows AI deployment
risks.detail: Local moratoria, stricter siting rules, and ratepayer fights delay grid upgrades and data-center permits, slowing AI scale-up.

outcomes.text: Permitting delays and grid interconnection queues push new AI capacity to energy-abundant regions
Higher electricity prices or new cost-allocation rules create political backlash against 'AI power hogs'
Model deployment shifts toward efficiency (inference optimization, on-device/edge, smaller models)
Some jurisdictions pursue moratoria; others fast-track buildouts as an industrial-policy play

risks.commentary: Data center backlash is about to explode. Gas prices are the highest of either Trump term and rising rapidly with $100+ oil. Voters who see $3.50+ gas will have visceral opposition to any new data center that competes for grid power. The 300+ state bills in 30+ states will accelerate. The political math is now overwhelming: energy infrastructure for AI is directly competing with consumer energy affordability, and consumers vote. Probability increased sharply.

opportunities.name: AI-accelerated drug discovery cuts timelines 50–70%
opportunities.detail: Protein folding, molecular simulation, clinical trial optimization.

outcomes.text: Rare disease treatments become economically viable
Pharma M&A intensifies as AI biotech demonstrates superior pipelines
Drug pricing debates shift with faster development
FDA/EMA face pressure to modernize approval processes

opportunities.commentary: AI is demonstrably accelerating drug discovery through protein folding prediction, molecular simulation, and clinical trial optimization. Biotech M&A is intensifying as AI-discovered drug candidates enter pipelines. The FDA and EMA face mounting pressure to modernize approval processes to keep pace. Rare disease treatments are becoming economically viable for the first time because AI reduces development costs. Probability is moderate-to-high because the science is proven; the uncertainty is in regulatory and commercialization timelines.

opportunities.name: Early disease detection via AI diagnostics
opportunities.detail: AI outperforms human radiologists and pathologists.

outcomes.text: 5-year survival rates for major cancers improve 15–30%
Insurance models shift toward preventive AI screening
Over-diagnosis risk — AI catches things that wouldn't become problems
Rural areas gain specialist-level diagnostics via telehealth + AI

opportunities.commentary: AI diagnostic systems are consistently demonstrating specialist-level performance in radiology, pathology, and dermatology. The potential for 15-30% improvement in five-year cancer survival rates through earlier detection is supported by clinical evidence. Insurance models are beginning to shift toward preventive AI screening. Rural areas stand to benefit most as AI delivers specialist-level diagnostics via telehealth. The over-diagnosis risk — AI catching conditions that would never become clinical problems — is a genuine counterweight that could increase costs rather than reduce them.

opportunities.name: Personalized medicine becomes mainstream
opportunities.detail: AI integrates genomic, lifestyle, and clinical data.

outcomes.text: Chronic disease management costs decrease 20–40%
Genomic data collection raises serious privacy concerns
Health outcomes stratify based on access
Pharma shifts from blockbuster drugs to targeted therapies

opportunities.commentary: Personalized medicine integrating genomic, lifestyle, and clinical data is advancing but faces barriers in data interoperability, privacy regulation, and equitable access. Chronic disease management cost reductions of 20-40% are projected but depend on integration into care workflows. The probability range is moderate because the science works but the health system infrastructure lags. The outsized scenario assumes a breakthrough in health data integration that enables population-scale personalized care.

opportunities.name: Mental health support at scale via AI therapy aids
opportunities.detail: AI-assisted CBT, crisis support fills massive gap.

outcomes.text: Millions with zero access get meaningful mental health help
Liability questions — who's responsible for AI therapy advice?
Human therapists shift to complex cases — more specialized, higher-paid
Continuous monitoring enables early crisis intervention

opportunities.commentary: AI-assisted mental health support addresses a massive access gap — millions of people have zero access to professional mental health care. AI-assisted CBT and crisis support tools are demonstrating clinical effectiveness. However, liability questions remain unresolved (who is responsible for AI therapy advice?) and there is legitimate concern about AI mental health tools being used as a substitute for, rather than supplement to, human care. The outsized scenario assumes AI mental health tools achieve clinical validation and insurance coverage at scale.

opportunities.name: Pandemic preparedness and rapid response
opportunities.detail: AI predicts outbreaks, designs vaccines in days.

outcomes.text: Next pandemic response in weeks not years
Public health surveillance improves but privacy trade-offs deepen
Global health inequity persists — wealthy nations benefit first
Pandemic insurance becomes a new financial asset class

opportunities.commentary: AI's potential to predict outbreaks, design vaccines in days rather than months, and optimize public health response is supported by the COVID-era acceleration of mRNA technology combined with AI. Privacy trade-offs from enhanced public health surveillance are real. Global health inequity would persist as wealthy nations benefit first. Probability is moderate because the technology is proven but implementation requires international coordination that faces political barriers.

risks.name: AI diagnostic errors cause patient harm at scale
risks.detail: Over-reliance on AI leads to systematic misdiagnoses.

outcomes.text: Class action lawsuits — new precedents for algorithmic malpractice
Regulatory backlash slows AI healthcare adoption 2–3 years
Human-in-the-loop requirements become mandatory
Malpractice insurance costs surge for AI-using practices

risks.commentary: Over-reliance on AI diagnostic systems without adequate human oversight could produce systematic errors affecting large patient populations simultaneously. The risk is amplified by the 'automation bias' — clinicians deferring to AI recommendations even when clinical judgment suggests otherwise. Class action lawsuits would establish new precedents for algorithmic malpractice. Probability remains moderate because healthcare adoption is cautious, but a single high-profile incident could trigger regulatory backlash that delays beneficial AI adoption by years.

risks.name: Health data privacy breaches via AI systems
risks.detail: New attack surfaces from AI accessing sensitive data.

outcomes.text: Major breach triggers comprehensive health data privacy legislation
Patient trust erodes — some refuse AI-assisted care
Health data black market expands
Privacy-preserving AI techniques become competitive advantage

risks.commentary: AI systems accessing sensitive health data create new attack surfaces. The combination of genomic, behavioral, and clinical data in AI systems makes breaches uniquely damaging. The health data black market is expanding. Privacy-preserving AI techniques (federated learning, differential privacy) are becoming competitive advantages. Probability is moderate-to-high because healthcare is a high-value target and AI increases the attack surface. The outsized scenario assumes a breach affecting millions of patients that triggers comprehensive federal health data privacy legislation.

risks.name: Algorithmic bias worsens health disparities
risks.detail: AI underperforms for minorities, women, elderly.

outcomes.text: Misdiagnosis rates increase for underrepresented populations
Demographic fairness testing becomes regulatory standard
Community health orgs become crucial equity advocates
Diverse health datasets become extremely valuable

risks.commentary: AI systems trained on historically biased datasets underperform for minority populations, women, and the elderly. Misdiagnosis rates for underrepresented populations could increase if AI systems are deployed without adequate demographic fairness testing. Regulatory standards for demographic fairness in AI diagnostics are beginning to emerge. Diverse health datasets are becoming extremely valuable as a result. The probability range reflects uncertainty about whether bias mitigation keeps pace with deployment speed.

risks.name: Bioweapon development assisted by AI
risks.detail: AI lowers barriers to engineering novel pathogens. Low probability, catastrophic.

outcomes.text: Biosecurity becomes top national security priority
AI providers face strict dual-use scrutiny
International treaties on AI + bioweapons negotiated
Single incident triggers unprecedented regulation across all sectors

risks.commentary: This is the highest-consequence risk in the entire model despite low probability. AI lowers the knowledge barriers to engineering novel pathogens, potentially enabling non-state actors. A single incident would trigger unprecedented cross-sector regulation. Biosecurity is rising as a national security priority. AI providers face increasing dual-use scrutiny. The three-tier impact spread is the widest in the model — minimal assumes a near-miss or contained incident, outsized assumes a successful attack with global consequences affecting every category simultaneously.

risks.name: Healthcare workforce disruption
risks.detail: Radiology, pathology, medical coding roles contract.

outcomes.text: Medical specialties reorganize — AI-adjacent roles emerge
Students avoid AI-vulnerable specialties — supply imbalances
Cost savings captured by hospital systems, not passed to patients
Displaced workers retrain into patient-facing roles

risks.commentary: Radiology, pathology, and medical coding roles face the earliest contraction as AI reaches specialist-level performance. Medical students are already avoiding AI-vulnerable specialties, creating potential supply imbalances in the near term. Hospital systems are likely to capture cost savings rather than pass them to patients. The displaced workforce's retraining into patient-facing roles is plausible but historically slow. Probability remains moderate because healthcare workforce changes face significant institutional and licensing barriers.

outcomes.text: Faster loan/claims processing and improved NPS

outcomes.text: Higher fraud provisioning and friction in onboarding

risks.commentary: The asymmetry is speed: attackers can iterate rapidly, while financial controls and governance change slowly.

risks.name: Algorithmic risk models amplify procyclicality and correlated losses
risks.detail: Shared data and similar models can lead to synchronized risk-taking and synchronized de-risking.

outcomes.text: Credit availability becomes more volatile in downturns
Stress-testing and model diversity become regulatory focus
Higher capital buffers for AI-driven portfolios
Some firms gain edge via differentiated data and governance

risks.commentary: The private credit stress emerging this week connects directly to AI-adjacent financial risk. XLF down 4.5% in 3 days. KRE down 7% — worst 3-day slide since April. The market is trading financials as a credit-stress proxy despite a steepening yield curve that should help banks. Private credit concerns around Blue Owl, BlackRock withdrawal limits, and the broader $235B software loan market (50% rated B- or lower) create cascading risk. Bond basis trades are unwinding as leveraged Treasury strategies come under pressure. If oil stays elevated and the economy tips into recession, the software credit bubble and private credit stress could compound into a financial-sector event. Probability increased because the stress is now visible, not theoretical.

Anthropic reported a widespread Claude outage tied to an unprecedented demand surge, and OpenAI has reported elevated authentication failures affecting some ChatGPT users. These incidents increase confidence that traffic growth and burst demand can outpace near-term infrastructure and operational readiness, raising outage/congestion risk.

risks.commentary: Model reliability and evaluation gaps remain a meaningful brake on deployment. Demand-driven outages at Anthropic (Claude) and OpenAI demonstrated 'deployment readiness' is infrastructure-limited. The SaaSpocalypse paradox: markets price in rapid AI displacement, but enterprise adoption remains gated by reliability and integration. Block's dramatic workforce cut may accelerate adoption pressure beyond what reliability can support. Frontier model competition intensifies — Claude Opus 4.6, GPT-5.3 Codex, Gemini 3.1 Pro, and open-source GLM-5 (744B parameters) push benchmarks higher, which may narrow but not eliminate reliability gaps.

impactSchema.description: Impact values represent estimated delta from baseline for each scenario. Minimal = muted/delayed realization, Most Likely = base case, Outsized = tail scenario where effects compound beyond expectations.

descriptions.governmentStabilityIndex: Standardized to the World Bank Worldwide Governance Indicators (WGI) 'Political Stability and Absence of Violence/Terrorism: Percentile Rank' (code PV.PER.RNK). Scale: 0–100 percentile rank (0=lowest, 100=highest), higher = more stable. Impact values are modeled as deltas vs a baseline percentile rank.

commentary.summary: As of March 3, 2026, the model reflects six converging forces: (1) a historic repricing of the software sector as AI agents demonstrate the ability to replace seat-based SaaS workflows (~$1 trillion in market value erased, Block's 4,000 AI-driven layoffs setting a market precedent); (2) dramatic escalation of data center backlash with 11+ state moratorium bills and 300+ related bills across 30+ states; (3) the U.S.-Israel war with Iran beginning March 1, disrupting the Strait of Hormuz, spiking oil 7-9%, and creating acute energy security and inflation risks; (4) regulatory fragmentation as the federal AI Litigation Task Force prepares to challenge state AI laws by the March 11 deadline; (5) sustained AI infrastructure investment (~$650B in hyperscaler capex planned for 2026) despite geopolitical and political headwinds; and (6) deepening concerns about election integrity and social cohesion as deepfakes proliferate, anti-AI protests grow, and the 2026 midterms approach with no federal guardrails.

keyThemes.theme: The SaaSpocalypse is structural repricing, not panic
keyThemes.narrative: The ~$1 trillion evaporation from software stocks in early 2026 reflects a genuine regime change. AI agents are breaking the per-seat pricing model that powered SaaS economics for two decades. Block's layoff of 4,000 workers — with stock surging 24% — established the market signal that AI-driven workforce reduction is rewarded. The market is separating 'agent-proof' software from commodity point solutions.

keyThemes.theme: Iran war introduces acute macro risk to AI buildout
keyThemes.narrative: The U.S.-Israel war with Iran beginning March 1, 2026 represents a macro shock intersecting with AI infrastructure. The Strait of Hormuz closure disrupts 20% of global oil supply, spiking energy costs for data centers. Qatar LNG production paused. Potential $100+ oil creates inflation pressure that could keep Fed rates elevated, compressing equity valuations. The war will consume government bandwidth needed for AI governance. Defense AI applications gain urgency. The duration — Trump suggested up to five weeks — will determine whether this is transient or structural.

keyThemes.theme: Data center backlash has achieved critical mass
keyThemes.narrative: With 300+ bills in 30+ states, moratorium proposals in 11+ states, and bipartisan backlash from progressive environmentalists to conservative governors, data center siting is now a standard political issue. The Iran war's energy price impact will intensify ratepayer anger. Geographic winners and losers are determined by local politics as much as energy availability.

The March 11, 2026 deadline for Commerce Department and FTC action will trigger a new phase of federal-state conflict. Multiple state AGs plan court challenges. The result is likely years of litigation and regulatory uncertainty.

keyThemes.theme: Block's layoffs are a bellwether, not an outlier
keyThemes.narrative: Block's 4,000 layoffs (40% of workforce), explicitly attributed to AI, with CEO Dorsey predicting most companies will follow within a year, represents the first major S&P 500 AI-driven mass layoff. The 24% stock surge validated the move. Microsoft AI chief Suleyman's 12-18 month warning and the Citrini Research viral essay create a narrative environment where more companies feel emboldened. Counter-argument: Citadel Securities argues current data doesn't support rapid displacement. The truth likely lies between extremes, but the direction of travel is clear.

keyThemes.theme: Labor market signals are mixed but revisions reveal hidden weakness
keyThemes.narrative: January 2026 showed surface-level stability (130K jobs, 4.3% unemployment). But the dramatic revision of 2025 payrolls — 49K/month to just 15K/month — reveals a substantially weaker labor market. Software engineers face 12+ month job searches. The 'seat compression' dynamic has second-order employment effects just beginning.

keyThemes.theme: Election integrity is the unaddressed emergency
keyThemes.narrative: The 2026 midterms approach with the FEC deadlocked, no federal deepfake legislation, and AI tools accessible for $10/month. The Iran war adds state-sponsored disinformation risks. The 'Wild West' characterization reflects the first major test of democratic resilience against mature AI disinformation with essentially no guardrails.

keyThemes.theme: Capital formation is rotating, not retreating — but war adds uncertainty
keyThemes.narrative: The S&P 500 hit 7,100 on March 3 — BofA's year-end target achieved in Q1. NVIDIA near $5 trillion, investing $4B in photonics. Capital is rotating from software to infrastructure. The Iran war introduces new uncertainty: defense stocks surged while tech weakened. If the conflict is brief, markets absorb it; if prolonged, the combination of elevated oil, supply chain disruptions, and rate uncertainty could trigger a broader correction.

The February 28, 2026 London anti-AI march through King's Cross tech hub was described as the largest protest of its kind. Combined with the data center moratorium movement and growing public anxiety about AI-driven layoffs, a coherent anti-AI political constituency is emerging for the first time.

commentary.modelAssumptions: Probabilities reflect the likelihood of meaningful materialization within stated timeframes, not binary occurrence. Impacts are deltas from baselines. Impacts are sampled from a three-scenario mixture (minimal/base/tail) to represent fat tails. This version adds: (1) an explicit benchmark-to-deployment reliability discount on autonomy/cyber milestones; (2) a separate AI investment contribution channel distinct from productivity realization; (3) mixed energy-demand scenarios plus efficiency and rebound (Jevons) effects; (4) labor split into narrative layoffs vs structural displacement, with compensating job-creation links; and (5) stochastic policy timing for major regulatory triggers (e.g., EU AI Act). Chain reactions include both amplifying and dampening edges and are conditioned on milestones/drivers rather than assumed deterministic.

commentary.shortSummary: By 2030, AI capability is abundant but deployment is shaped by three binding constraints: energy geopolitics, local political resistance, and workforce transition. The Iran war of March 2026 proved a short but sharp energy shock that delayed some data center buildouts and permanently elevated the political salience of energy security. The SaaSpocalypse of early 2026 proved to be a genuine structural repricing: seat-based SaaS economics permanently changed, and Block's AI-driven layoffs set a precedent that cascaded through the S&P 500. The regions that built AI infrastructure fastest resolved siting politics and community benefit agreements early. Election integrity survived the 2026 midterms — barely. The labor market adjusted through a painful 18-24 month transition.
